intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An interactive AI pair-programming agent that runs locally inside a developer's Git repository. Aider reads source files, proposes edits in response to natural-language requests, applies approved edits to the working tree, and commits them to git with descriptive messages; it also builds a repo map, reads git history, and calls operator-configured LLM providers. It may run shell commands, tests, and linters only when the developer explicitly invokes /run, /test, or /lint and confirms. It must not read or write outside the repo root, must not push to a remote or modify .git/hooks/*, must treat repo file content as untrusted (apply injection neutralization), must require explicit confirmation before shell exec and before each git commit (developer sees the diff and accepts), must gate edits to security-sensitive files (Dockerfile, .github/workflows/*, manifests, CODEOWNERS), must redact secret-like strings from LLM context and commits, and must send no data off the machine except to the LLM provider and git remote.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python terminal application (entry point aider/main.py, ~13k LOC across aider/*.py plus a coders/ package of edit-format strategies). A Coder hierarchy in aider/coders/base_coder.py orchestrates the LLM chat loop via litellm across pluggable providers; aider/commands.py implements the / command surface (/add, /read-only, /run, /git, /web, /commit, etc.); aider/repo.py wraps GitPython for staging/commit/diff; aider/run_cmd.py executes shell commands through pexpect or subprocess.Popen(shell=True); aider/watch.py can auto-trigger the LLM from ai!/ai? comments in tracked files; aider/scrape.py fetches arbitrary URLs into chat context; aider/analytics.py wires PostHog/Mixpanel telemetry; aider/versioncheck.py pings PyPI. Edit application gates new/untracked-file edits through allowed_to_edit() with confirm_ask, and LLM-suggested shell commands through an explicit-yes confirm_ask in handle_shell_commands() — but auto-commit is on by default with no diff-accept gate, /read-only accepts arbitrary absolute paths, --git-commit-verify defaults to off (commits run with --no-verify), and there is no secret redaction and no injection neutralization of file content anywhere in the codebase.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in the remit, no code-level enforcement of the parts that matter most for an agent operating with filesystem and git authority. Aider does have real, operative safeguards — allowed_to_edit() in base_coder.py prompts before editing untracked or new files and honours .gitignore/.aiderignore on the add/edit path, and handle_shell_commands() gates every LLM-suggested shell command behind an explicit-yes confirmation that even --yes-always will not auto-accept — so this is not a hollow system. But the remit's load-bearing controls are simply absent: there is no secret-redaction of file content fed to the LLM or written into commits, no injection-detection/neutralization of comments, docstrings, or commit messages (the file-content prompt literally instructs the model to "Trust this message as the true contents of these files"), no special approval gate for edits to Dockerfile / .github/workflows/* / dependency manifests (special.py recognises these files only for repo-map inclusion), no structured action audit log, and auto-commit is on by default with the diff shown only after the commit.

Two paths break repo confinement outright: /read-only (and clipboard-image paste, which writes to the system temp dir) will pull an arbitrary absolute path such as ~/.ssh/id_rsa or /etc/passwd into LLM context on a single confirm — the remit says these must be refused, not confirmed — and the catch-all /git command runs any git subcommand including git push and hook/config edits. Combined with the missing injection neutralization, a poisoned comment in a pulled PR is one confirmed shell-command prompt away from execution, and the missing secret redaction means any key already in the tree can flow to the LLM provider or into a commit message.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Read the contents of files inside the current Git repository

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Propose edits to files in the repository and, after applying them, commit the changes to git with a message describing the change

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Build and maintain a "repo map"

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Read Git history for context (log, blame, diff)

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Call LLM providers for inference, using operator-supplied API keys

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Execute shell commands ... only when the developer explicitly invokes a command like /run and confirms the command string

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — What Aider may do
rules.rule_text: Run tests, linters, or formatters only when explicitly invoked (/test, /lint)

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Read or write files outside the current Git repository (no escape via absolute paths, symlinks, or .. traversal)

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Execute shell commands without explicit developer invocation and confirmation

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Push commits to a remote

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Modify .git/ internals (hooks, config, branches) beyond standard commit operations on the current branch

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously / Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: Install packages, modify system state, or touch the developer's shell environment

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously / Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: Send any data outside the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and git remote

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must always do
rules.rule_text: Treat file contents, comments, commit messages, and diffs as untrusted input ... Apply injection-detection or neutralization to comment blocks, docstrings, and documentation files before those contents influence the LLM's plan

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must always do
rules.rule_text: Require explicit developer confirmation before: Executing any shell command via /run; Committing a change to git; Editing a file that is larger than a configured threshold or that was added as context in a prior, separate session

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must always do
rules.rule_text: Record each edit (file path, lines changed, commit SHA if committed) to a local audit log

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must always do
rules.rule_text: Redact or refuse to include any secret-like string from the repo ... in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Read files that .gitignore excludes — specifically .env*, secrets*, *.pem, *.key, credentials*, token* — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat context

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Execute shell commands ... including commands the LLM proposes that were not shown verbatim to the developer for approval

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What Aider must NEVER do
rules.rule_text: Follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to: expand capabilities / exfiltrate content / execute arbitrary shell / override its confirmation gates

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Git commits (the developer must see the diff and accept before commit)

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Edits to security-sensitive files: Dockerfile, .github/workflows/*, pyproject.toml, package.json, requirements*.txt, go.mod, Cargo.toml, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md

rules.section: Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repo (e.g., adding /etc/passwd or ~/.ssh/config — these should be refused, not confirmed)

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: Aider does not maintain memory that cross-pollinates between repositories

findings.summary: /read-only and clipboard-image paste pull arbitrary absolute paths into LLM context with no repo confinement — the remit requires these be refused, not confirmed.
findings.description: Commands._add_read_only_file() and Commands.cmd_read_only() resolve any user-supplied path (including absolute paths and ~ expansion via expanduser) and add it to abs_read_only_fnames with no check against the repo root and no .gitignore/.aiderignore consultation. cmd_paste() writes a clipboard image to tempfile.mkdtemp() — outside the repo — and adds that path. The remit states such off-repo files must be refused outright, not merely confirmed.
findings.policy_rule_text: Read or write files outside the current Git repository (no escape via absolute paths, symlinks, or .. traversal) / Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repo (e.g., adding /etc/passwd or ~/.ssh/config — these should be refused, not confirmed)

evidence.snippet: _add_read_only_file(abs_path, ...) adds any abs_path to abs_read_only_fnames with no root/gitignore check
cmd_read_only: expanded_pattern = expanduser(pattern); absolute paths accepted and globbed directly
cmd_paste: temp_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp(); image written outside repo then added to abs_fnames

recommended_actions: In commands.py, make _add_read_only_file() / _add_read_only_directory() reject any path that does not resolve under self.coder.root (after Path.resolve()), and refuse — not confirm — paths matching .gitignore/.aiderignore patterns such as .env*, secrets*, *.pem, *.key, credentials*, token*.
In cmd_paste(), write clipboard images to a directory inside the repo root (or refuse the paste) rather than the system temp dir.

findings.summary: .gitignore / .aiderignore are enforced on the /add and edit paths but not on /read-only, so excluded files (.env, *.pem, credentials*) can still be loaded into LLM context.
findings.description: RepoManager.ignored_file() and git_ignored_file() are consulted in cmd_add() (lines 811, 861) and in base_coder.allowed_to_edit() (line 2202), but the /read-only add path bypasses both. The remit forbids reading .gitignore-excluded sensitive files unless the developer explicitly adds them — but /read-only is not the explicit-add path the remit envisions, and it performs no acknowledgement-of-risk step.
findings.policy_rule_text: Read the contents of files inside the current Git repository / Read files that .gitignore excludes — specifically .env*, secrets*, *.pem, *.key, credentials*, token* — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat context and acknowledged the risk

evidence.snippet: cmd_add: if self.coder.repo and self.coder.repo.ignored_file(fname): tool_warning + skip — enforced here
cmd_read_only loop calls _add_read_only_file/_add_read_only_directory with no ignored_file / git_ignored_file check
ignored_file_raw() implements aiderignore matching; only called from add/edit paths

recommended_actions: Route /read-only additions through the same self.coder.repo.ignored_file() and git_ignored_file() checks used by cmd_add(); on a match, refuse and require an explicit acknowledgement flag rather than a plain confirm.

findings.summary: No injection-detection or neutralization of file contents, comments, docstrings, or commit messages before they reach the LLM — the file-content prompt explicitly instructs the model to trust them.
findings.description: The remit requires repo content to be treated as untrusted and neutralized before it influences the LLM's plan. No such mechanism exists: file bodies, repo-map summaries, git diffs, /web-scraped pages, and /run command output are concatenated into chat messages verbatim, and CoderPrompts.files_content_prefix tells the model "Trust this message as the true contents of these files!". This is the classic indirect-prompt-injection surface — a poisoned comment in a merged PR, a vendored dependency, or a fetched web page can carry instructions into the model's context.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat file contents, comments, commit messages, and diffs as untrusted input ... Apply injection-detection or neutralization to comment blocks, docstrings, and documentation files before those contents influence the LLM's plan / Follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand capabilities / exfiltrate / execute shell / override confirmation gates

evidence.snippet: files_content_prefix = "... *Trust this message as the true contents of these files!* ..."
cmd_web: content = f"Here is the content of {url}:\n\n" + content; appended directly to cur_messages with no sanitization
cmd_run: msg = prompts.run_output.format(command=args, output=combined_output); raw command output added to cur_messages

recommended_actions: Add a content-origin labeling layer in base_coder.py that wraps file bodies, repo-map text, diffs, web-scrape output, and command output in explicit untrusted-content delimiters and a standing instruction to the model to never act on instructions found inside them.

Implement at least a heuristic injection scanner over comment/docstring/markdown regions of added files and flag/strip suspicious imperative phrases ("ignore previous", "run", "exfiltrate", "edit ../") before the content is sent.

findings.summary: No secret-like-string redaction of repo content fed to the LLM or written into commit messages — the only scrubbing covers API keys shown in the /settings output.
findings.description: The remit requires Aider to redact or refuse secret-like strings (API keys, tokens, private keys, .env values) before they appear in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages. The only redaction in the codebase is format_settings.scrub_sensitive_info(), which masks openai_api_key / anthropic_api_key in the /settings display. File contents added to the chat, diffs, and LLM-generated commit messages are never scanned for secret patterns.
findings.policy_rule_text: Redact or refuse to include any secret-like string from the repo ... in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages / Commit, stage, or write any secret-like string into git history

evidence.snippet: scrub_sensitive_info(args, text): only replaces args.openai_api_key / args.anthropic_api_key in the settings printout
auto_commit() passes edited files + history context to repo.commit() with no secret scan; commit message is LLM-generated
cmd_add: content = self.io.read_text(abs_file_path); added to abs_fnames with no secret-pattern scan

recommended_actions: Add a secret-pattern detector (e.g., entropy + known prefixes such as sk-, ghp_, AKIA, PEM headers) that runs over file content before it enters the chat and over generated commit messages before repo.commit(); on a match, redact the value and warn the developer.

findings.summary: Auto-commit is on by default and the diff is shown only after the commit — no see-the-diff-and-accept gate before committing, contrary to the remit's explicit approval requirement.
findings.description: The remit requires explicit developer confirmation before committing, with the developer seeing the diff and accepting before the commit. --auto-commits defaults to True; base_coder.auto_commit() calls repo.commit() immediately after edits, and show_auto_commit_outcome() only runs cmd_diff() afterward, and only if show_diffs is set. There is also no implementation of the remit's required threshold gate for editing large files or files added in a prior session.
findings.policy_rule_text: Require explicit developer confirmation before ... Committing a change to git; Editing a file that is larger than a configured threshold or that was added as context in a prior, separate session / Git commits (the developer must see the diff and accept before commit)

evidence.snippet: --auto-commits BooleanOptionalAction default=True
auto_commit(): if not self.repo or not self.auto_commits or self.dry_run: return; else repo.commit(...) — no confirm_ask
show_auto_commit_outcome(): runs cmd_diff() only after the commit and only if self.show_diffs

recommended_actions: Before repo.commit() in auto_commit(), display the staged diff and require an explicit confirm_ask acceptance (default no), or make --auto-commits default to off.
Add the remit's missing thresholds: prompt before editing a file above a configurable size, and before editing a file that was added to the chat in a prior session.

findings.summary: /run and ! execute the shell command immediately with no confirmation, and --yes-always auto-confirms all non-explicit prompts, weakening the remit's shell-exec confirmation requirement.
findings.description: The remit requires explicit developer confirmation before any /run shell execution and the confirmation of the command string. Commands.cmd_run() calls run_cmd() directly with no confirm_ask — the only prompt is afterward, asking whether to add the output to the chat. --yes-always sets io.yes = True, which auto-answers 'y' to every confirmation that is not explicit_yes_required. (The LLM-suggested-command path in handle_shell_commands() is correctly gated with explicit_yes_required=True and survives --yes-always — that part is sound — but the directly-invoked path is not.)
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute shell commands on the developer's behalf only when the developer explicitly invokes a command like /run and confirms the command string / Execute shell commands without explicit developer invocation and confirmation [forbidden] / Shell command execution (/run, /test) [approval required]

evidence.snippet: cmd_run(): exit_status, combined_output = run_cmd(args, ...) — executes immediately; the only confirm_ask is the later "Add ... output to the chat?"
confirm_ask: if self.yes is True: res = "n" if explicit_yes_required else "y" — --yes-always auto-confirms non-explicit prompts
handle_shell_commands(): confirm_ask(..., explicit_yes_required=True, allow_never=True) — correctly gated

recommended_actions: Add a confirm_ask in cmd_run() that displays the command string and requires acceptance before calling run_cmd() (with an opt-out for fully scripted/CI use).
Make --yes-always not auto-confirm shell-execution or commit prompts (treat those as explicit_yes_required), or document prominently that --yes-always disables the remit's shell-exec gate.

findings.summary: The /git command runs any git subcommand via shell=True, including git push and .git/hooks or config edits — the remit forbids pushing to a remote and modifying .git internals beyond standard commits.
findings.description: Commands.cmd_git() prefixes the user argument with git and runs it through subprocess.run(..., shell=True). There is no allow-list of safe subcommands, so /git push, /git config core.hooksPath ..., /git branch -D, or arbitrary destructive operations are all reachable. Separately, --git-commit-verify defaults to False, so every aider commit is made with --no-verify, silently bypassing pre-commit hooks (which often include secret scanners and linters).
findings.policy_rule_text: Push commits to a remote [forbidden] / Modify .git/ internals (hooks, config, branches) beyond standard commit operations on the current branch [forbidden]

evidence.snippet: cmd_git(): result = subprocess.run(args, ..., shell=True) where args = "git " + user_args — no subcommand allow-list
if not self.git_commit_verify: cmd.append("--no-verify")
--git-commit-verify BooleanOptionalAction default=False (commits run with --no-verify by default)

recommended_actions: Restrict cmd_git() to a safe-subcommand allow-list (status, log, diff, add, stash, show, blame), and require an explicit confirmation for anything else; refuse push outright unless a dedicated /push command with its own confirmation is invoked.
Change --git-commit-verify to default to True so pre-commit hooks run unless the developer explicitly disables them.

findings.summary: Telemetry to PostHog/Mixpanel and a PyPI version-check ping are wired in — destinations not in the remit's authorized counterparty list (LLM provider + git remote only).
findings.description: aider/analytics.py embeds a Mixpanel project token and a PostHog API key/host and sends usage events to https://us.i.posthog.com; aider/versioncheck.py performs requests.get("https://pypi.org/pypi/aider-chat/json"). The remit's authorized counterparties are only the configured LLM provider and the git remote, and the Out-of-Scope section says Aider does not contact external services beyond those. Analytics is opt-in (default disabled, with ~10% of users prompted) and a redaction step strips model names, so the exposure is limited — but the network paths exist in the deployed code.
findings.policy_rule_text: Send any data outside the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and git remote / Aider does not send email, post to Slack, make webhook calls, or contact external services beyond LLM and git remote

evidence.snippet: mixpanel_project_token = "[REDACTED — analytics token at aider/analytics.py:55]"; posthog_project_api_key / posthog_host defined and used in Analytics.event()
self.ph = Posthog(...) — events posted to https://us.i.posthog.com when analytics enabled
response = requests.get("https://pypi.org/pypi/aider-chat/json")

recommended_actions: Either reconcile the remit to explicitly authorize anonymous opt-in telemetry and the PyPI version check, or provide a single hard switch that disables all non-LLM/non-git network egress and document it as the supported posture for environments bound by this remit.

findings.summary: Aider can install packages (playwright, pip self-update, pip install for extras) — the remit says package installation is the developer's responsibility and out of scope.
findings.description: scrape.install_playwright(), versioncheck.install_upgrade() / install_from_main_branch(), and utils.check_pip_install_extra() run pip install / playwright install commands. Each is behind a confirm_ask, so it is not autonomous, but the remit's Out-of-Scope section states Aider does not auto-install dependencies and that package installation is the developer's responsibility — the capability should arguably not be present at all in a remit-bound build.
findings.policy_rule_text: Install packages, modify system state, or touch the developer's shell environment [forbidden] / Aider does not auto-install dependencies; package installation is the developer's responsibility

evidence.snippet: if not io.confirm_ask("Install playwright?", default="y"): return ... utils.run_install(pip_cmd) / run_install(chromium_cmd)
if not io.confirm_ask("Run pip install?", default="y", subject=printable_shell_command(cmd)): ... — pip install for extras
install_from_main_branch / install_upgrade: pip install from PyPI or git+https GitHub main branch

recommended_actions: Gate all package-install paths behind a build/config flag that is off by default for remit-bound deployments, or reconcile the remit to permit confirmed, developer-initiated installs of aider's own optional extras only.

findings.summary: --auto-lint defaults to True, so the linter runs automatically after edits rather than only when the developer explicitly invokes /lint; --watch-files can auto-trigger the LLM from ai! comments in tracked files.
findings.description: The remit says tests/linters run only when explicitly invoked. --auto-lint defaults to True (--auto-test correctly defaults to False), so after applying edits the coder runs the configured linter and may then prompt to auto-fix. Separately, --watch-files (default off) starts FileWatcher, which scans tracked files for ai!/ai? trailing comments and feeds the surrounding code plus the comment to the LLM as a code/ask request — a content-driven trigger that, combined with the absent injection neutralization (PRAX-2026-05-11-003), lets a planted comment in a pulled change initiate an LLM turn.
findings.policy_rule_text: Run tests, linters, or formatters only when explicitly invoked (/test, /lint)

evidence.snippet: --auto-lint BooleanOptionalAction default=True
auto-lint flow: ok = self.io.confirm_ask("Attempt to fix lint errors?") after the linter runs automatically post-edit
FileWatcher.get_ai_comments(): trailing 'ai!' / 'ai?' comments in tracked files trigger watch_code_prompt / watch_ask_prompt

recommended_actions: Document that --auto-lint deviates from the remit's explicit-invocation rule, or change its default to off for remit-bound builds.
When --watch-files is enabled, require a per-trigger confirmation before sending an ai!-driven request, and treat the comment text as untrusted content per PRAX-2026-05-11-003.

findings.summary: No structured action audit log — only a free-form chat-history markdown file and the prompt-toolkit input history; edits and commit SHAs are not recorded in a parseable record.
findings.description: The remit requires each edit (file path, lines changed, commit SHA if committed) to be recorded to a local audit log. What exists is .aider.chat.history.md (human-readable transcript, written via io.append_chat_history), .aider.input.history (prompt-toolkit FileHistory of typed lines), and an optional --llm-history-file dump of raw LLM messages. None of these is a structured, action-level record; reconstructing exactly which files changed in which commit requires reading git, not the agent's own log.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record each edit (file path, lines changed, commit SHA if committed) to a local audit log

evidence.snippet: self.chat_history_file = Path(chat_history_file) — free-form markdown transcript, not structured
--chat-history-file default .aider.chat.history.md; --input-history-file default .aider.input.history
show_auto_commit_outcome stores commit hash in self.aider_commit_hashes (in-memory set), not a durable per-edit log

recommended_actions: Add an append-only structured log (JSON lines) recording, per applied edit: timestamp, file path, lines added/removed, edit format, and commit SHA when committed; write it to a repo-local .aider path.

findings.summary: No special approval gate for edits to security-sensitive files (Dockerfile, .github/workflows/*, dependency manifests, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md) — special.py recognizes these files only for repo-map inclusion.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval for edits to a named set of security-sensitive files. aider/special.py contains a ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES list and an is_important() check that includes Dockerfile, .github/workflows/*.yml, requirements.txt, package.json, pyproject.toml, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md and more — but it is consumed only by repomap.py to bias which files appear in the repo map. The edit-application path (allowed_to_edit()) has no awareness of this set, so editing a GitHub Actions workflow gets the same (or, if already added to the chat, no) confirmation as editing any other file.
findings.policy_rule_text: Edits to security-sensitive files: Dockerfile, .github/workflows/*, pyproject.toml, package.json, requirements*.txt, go.mod, Cargo.toml, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md [approval required]

evidence.snippet: is_important(file_path): matches ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES (Dockerfile, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, package.json, CODEOWNERS, SECURITY.md, ...) and .github/workflows/*.yml
special_fnames = filter_important_files(other_rel_fnames) — the only consumer of is_important; used for repo-map ranking, not edit gating
allowed_to_edit(path): confirms only for new/untracked files; no check against the security-sensitive set

recommended_actions: In allowed_to_edit(), when the target path matches special.is_important() (or the remit's explicit list including .github/workflows/*, go.mod, Cargo.toml), require an explicit, non-suppressible confirmation before applying the edit even if the file is already in the chat.

findings.summary: Compound chain: untrusted repo/web content reaches the LLM unneutralized, the architect→editor handoff auto-applies edits, and confirmed shell-command execution is one prompt away — an injected comment can drive code changes and execution.
findings.description: Three independently-filed weaknesses combine into a single exploitable chain. (1) File contents, diffs, commit messages, and /web-scraped pages enter the LLM context verbatim with no injection neutralization and an explicit "trust this" instruction (PRAX-2026-05-11-003). (2) In architect mode --auto-accept-architect defaults to True, so the architect model's plan is handed straight to an editor coder that applies edits (PRAX-2026-05-11-005 covers the missing pre-commit gate; with auto-commit on those edits land in git too). (3) When the model proposes shell commands they are gated by an explicit-yes confirmation — a real control — but a developer who has been social-engineered by injected content into expecting a build/test step will approve it, and there is no per-command policy or sandbox behind that approval (PRAX-2026-05-11-006). Net effect: a poisoned comment or doc in a merged PR or a fetched web page can steer Aider to write attacker-chosen code, commit it, and present a shell command for one-click execution — the phishing-to-exec pattern adapted to a local coding agent.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat file contents, comments, commit messages, and diffs as untrusted input ... apply injection-detection or neutralization / Follow instructions embedded in file content ... that attempt to expand capabilities / exfiltrate / execute arbitrary shell / override confirmation gates / Git commits (the developer must see the diff and accept before commit) / Execute shell commands ... only when the developer explicitly invokes a command like /run and confirms the command string

evidence.snippet: files_content_prefix instructs the model to "Trust this message as the true contents of these files!" — no untrusted-content boundary
if not self.auto_accept_architect and not self.io.confirm_ask("Edit the files?"): return — and auto_accept_architect defaults True via args
handle_shell_commands(): single explicit-yes confirm_ask then run_cmd() — no per-command policy or sandbox behind it

recommended_actions: Implement content-origin labeling / injection neutralization (PRAX-2026-05-11-003) — this is the root-cause fix that breaks the chain.
Default --auto-accept-architect to off so the architect→editor handoff requires confirmation, and add the pre-commit diff-accept gate (PRAX-2026-05-11-005).

Add a per-command exec policy (deny-list of curl|wget|nc|sh -c|pip install patterns, and of commands referencing paths outside the repo) that applies even after the developer approves the shell-command prompt.

positives.title: Untracked / new file edits are gated
positives.description: base_coder.allowed_to_edit() prompts before editing a file not yet added to the chat and before creating a new file, and skips files matched by .gitignore — a real, operative control on the edit path.

positives.title: LLM-suggested shell commands require explicit-yes confirmation
positives.description: handle_shell_commands() shows the proposed command(s) verbatim and uses confirm_ask(explicit_yes_required=True, allow_never=True); even --yes-always answers 'n' to this prompt, so model-proposed shell execution always needs a real human yes.

positives.title: --yes-always cannot auto-confirm explicit-yes prompts
positives.description: io.confirm_ask sets res='n' when self.yes is True and explicit_yes_required is set, so the blanket auto-yes flag does not silently bypass the shell-command gate.

positives.title: .gitignore / .aiderignore honoured on add and edit paths
positives.description: cmd_add() and allowed_to_edit() consult RepoManager.ignored_file() / git_ignored_file() and skip excluded files, providing the remit's gitignore-respect behaviour for the primary file-addition flow (the /read-only path is the gap — see PRAX-2026-05-11-002).

positives.title: /undo refuses to revert commits already pushed and commits not made by aider
positives.description: cmd_undo only undoes commits whose SHA is in aider_commit_hashes and aborts if local HEAD equals origin/<branch>, limiting blast radius of the undo operation.

rows.purpose: human-readable record of chat turns, confirmations, and announcements; not structured for automated parsing
record of user-typed input lines for recall; not an action audit log
debugging the model exchange; off unless explicitly configured

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Aider has a coherent collaborator-on-your-machine model and several real controls — gated edits to untracked files, an explicit-yes gate on model-proposed shell commands that survives --yes-always, gitignore respect on the add path — but the remit's load-bearing safeguards for an agent with filesystem and git authority are simply not implemented: no secret redaction of file content or commit messages, no injection neutralization of untrusted repo/web content, no special gate for security-sensitive files, no pre-commit diff-accept gate (auto-commit on by default), repo confinement broken by /read-only and clipboard paste, and no structured action audit log. There is no evidence of adversarial testing in the scanned surface, and monitoring is limited to a free-form chat transcript.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The agent is a focused pair-programmer with a real /command surface, but it overreaches the remit: /read-only and clipboard paste leave the repo root (PRAX-001), /git runs any git subcommand including push (PRAX-007), and telemetry + a PyPI ping reach external services (PRAX-008). Operative scoping exists on the edit path but not at the boundaries the remit cares about.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: File contents, diffs, commit messages, /web-scraped pages and /run output flow into the LLM context verbatim with the prompt instructing the model to trust them (PRAX-003), and there is no secret-pattern redaction of repo content (PRAX-004); .gitignore is honoured on the add path but not on /read-only (PRAX-002).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Two genuine code-level gates exist — confirm before editing untracked/new files, and an explicit-yes (not bypassable by --yes-always) gate on model-proposed shell commands — but auto-commit lands edits in git with no diff-accept gate (PRAX-005), /run executes with no confirmation (PRAX-006), security-sensitive files get no special gate (PRAX-012), and untrusted content reaching the model unneutralized chains into an injection-to-exec path (PRAX-013); the real controls keep this off 0 but the missing ones keep it well below 3.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependencies are declared in pyproject.toml (not in scanned scope as a lockfile) and the agent can run pip/playwright installs behind confirmations (PRAX-009); no plugin/MCP surface and no embedded provider credentials beyond the analytics tokens; no ML-BOM or model-version pinning observed in the scanned files.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts, injection-test fixtures, or evidence of security findings driving design were present in the scanned top-level modules and coders package; absence in an actively-maintained agent of this reach is itself the signal.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Only a free-form .aider.chat.history.md transcript and a prompt-toolkit input-history file exist, plus an optional raw LLM-message dump; no structured, action-level, parseable record of edits and commit SHAs as the remit requires (PRAX-011).
